segments.text:
Hi, today I'm going to show you how you can detect
secrets before they get leaked and result
in security incidents.

I'm going to walk you through a demo where
I'll show you how you can use SonarQube CLI
to detect secrets.

And if you're a Claude Code user, you definitely
want to see how the SonarQube CLI can help you.

AI coding agents read your files to build context.
That includes config files, ENV files, and logs.

If any of those contain a live secret,
it goes straight to the LLM.

You're not going to get a warning.
The agent doesn't know that it's doing anything wrong,
but the SonarQube CLI fixes that.

I'll set it up in a couple minutes.
So you start with one curl command to install.

Okay. And we're also going to want to export that path.
The installer updates your shell profile,
but you need to reload the path for the current session.

The next thing you need to do is authenticate your CLI
to connect to SonarQube Cloud or server.

SonarQube Cloud is an automatic code review
and verification solution. If you don't have access
to SonarQube Cloud, you can start a free trial
at sonar.com.

Now run the secret scanner. This is a separate binary
that the CLI manages for you and covers over
400 secret patterns across 248 cloud services.

So here's a database config with hardcoded passwords,
two of them in plain text.
So we're going to want to scan it.

So we do that with
sonar analyze secrets
and then the file name.

Two secrets were found.
Exit code 51 means secrets detected.
That's not an error.

That's the scanner doing its job.
Any CI pipeline or hook can key off
that exit code to block.

Now if we do the same thing for a clean file
that has the same structure.

But secrets are in environment variables
where they belong.

So same thing —
run sonar analyze secrets on that file.
Clean. The scanner completed successfully.

Now here's the real payoff.
We can use one command to protect Claude Code.

This installs two hooks into your project.
One scans every file before Claude reads it.

The other scans every prompt you submit.
If either finds a secret, it blocks it.
The secret never hits the LLM.

Both hooks live right here in your project settings,
although you can also install them globally
with a flag.

Zero workflow changes.
And that's it.

Every file read, every prompt scanned automatically.
Secrets stay local. The SonarQube CLI is in open beta
right now. Link in the description.

fullText:
Hi, today I'm going to show you how you can detect
secrets before they get leaked and result
in security incidents. I'm going to walk you through a demo where
I'll show you how you can use SonarQube CLI
to detect secrets. And if you're a Claude Code user, you definitely
want to see how the SonarQube CLI can help you. AI coding agents read your files to build context.
That includes config files, ENV files, and logs. If any of those contain a live secret,
it goes straight to the LLM. You're not going to get a warning.
The agent doesn't know that it's doing anything wrong,
but the SonarQube CLI fixes that. I'll set it up in a couple minutes.
So you start with one curl command to install. Okay. And we're also going to want to export that path.
The installer updates your shell profile,
but you need to reload the path for the current session. The next thing you need to do is authenticate your CLI
to connect to SonarQube Cloud or server. SonarQube Cloud is an automatic code review
and verification solution. If you don't have access
to SonarQube Cloud, you can start a free trial
at sonar.com. Now run the secret scanner. This is a separate binary
that the CLI manages for you and covers over
400 secret patterns across 248 cloud services. So here's a database config with hardcoded passwords,
two of them in plain text.
So we're going to want to scan it. So we do that with
sonar analyze secrets
and then the file name. Two secrets were found.
Exit code 51 means secrets detected.
That's not an error. That's the scanner doing its job.
Any CI pipeline or hook can key off
that exit code to block. Now if we do the same thing for a clean file
that has the same structure. But secrets are in environment variables
where they belong. So same thing —
run sonar analyze secrets on that file. Clean. The scanner completed successfully. Now here's the real payoff.
We can use one command to protect Claude Code. This installs two hooks into your project.
One scans every file before Claude reads it. The other scans every prompt you submit.
If either finds a secret, it blocks it.
The secret never hits the LLM. Both hooks live right here in your project settings,
although you can also install them globally
with a flag. Zero workflow changes.
And that's it. Every file read, every prompt scanned automatically.
Secrets stay local. The SonarQube CLI is in open beta
right now. Link in the description.
